parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded tank. This is indicated by the rusty brown water visible in the drip tray and the leak from a fitting, which is often a secondary symptom of internal tank degradation. The symptoms you described, such as higher electricity bills and hot water not lasting as long, are also consistent with a corroding tank.

This condition typically develops when the internal sacrificial anode, designed to protect the geyser's steel tank from rust, has been fully consumed. Once the anode is gone, the tank's steel walls begin to corrode, leading to rust in the water and eventual leaks.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, leading to more significant leaks and potential structural failure of the tank. This could result in a burst geyser, causing extensive water damage to your property.

There is a risk of electrical shorting if water comes into contact with the geyser's electrical components. For your safety, it is advisable to switch off the geyser's power at your distribution board.

This close-up image focuses on the copper pipework and fittings, revealing rust and white mineral deposits around the connections. A white flexible pipe is also visible, running alongside the copper pipe.

This image provides an external view of a residential building with open windows and a brick wall. It offers environmental context but does not show any specific components related to the geyser issue.

Assess the overall condition of the geyser and its associated plumbing, including the pressure control valve.
Recommend a suitable course of action, which will likely involve replacing the geyser unit.
Drain and safely remove the old geyser, then install and plumb in a new unit, ensuring all connections are secure and leak-free.

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing or broken spring on one side. This spring is crucial for counterbalancing the door's weight, allowing the motor to lift it smoothly or for you to open it manually with ease.

Garage door springs are under immense tension and are designed to provide the necessary lift assistance. When one breaks or goes missing, the entire weight of the door shifts, causing it to become unbalanced and extremely heavy. This imbalance puts excessive strain on the remaining components, including the motor and lifting cables.

Operating the door with a missing or broken spring can cause further damage to the motor, cables, tracks, and the door panels themselves. It can also be very dangerous, as the door could fall unexpectedly.
image_descriptions: This image shows the left side of the garage door, clearly revealing the absence of a spring mechanism where one would typically be attached to the frame. The door itself is visibly sagging on this side.

A full view of the garage door from inside, illustrating its significant tilt and imbalance, with the left side hanging lower than the right. The ceiling-mounted motor unit and its connecting arm are visible in the upper centre.

A close-up of the motor's connecting arm attached to the top of the garage door, showing the emergency release cord. This image highlights the central attachment point without revealing the side spring mechanisms.
A detailed shot of the bracket connecting the motor's arm to the top panel of the garage door. The cotter pin securing the connection is clearly visible in this frame.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows the left side of the garage door, clearly revealing the absence of a spring mechanism where one would typically be attached to the frame. The door itself is visibly sagging on this side.

A full view of the garage door from inside, illustrating its significant tilt and imbalance, with the left side hanging lower than the right. The ceiling-mounted motor unit and its connecting arm are visible in the upper centre.

A close-up of the motor's connecting arm attached to the top of the garage door, showing the emergency release cord. This image highlights the central attachment point without revealing the side spring mechanisms.
A detailed shot of the bracket connecting the motor's arm to the top panel of the garage door. The cotter pin securing the connection is clearly visible in this frame.
contractor_checklist: Inspect the entire garage door system for any additional damage caused by the unbalanced operation.
Replace the missing or broken spring with a new one matched to the door's weight and specifications.
Rebalance the garage door to ensure smooth and safe operation.
Adjust and lubricate all moving parts, including hinges, rollers, and tracks.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Keep the garage door disengaged from the motor and avoid attempting to open or close it manually until a technician arrives.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy and drops quickly, the spring system is compromised.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'The spring is missing on one side'.
Image 1 clearly shows the absence of a spring mechanism on the left side.
Image 2 shows the garage door is severely tilted and unbalanced.
Visible imbalance is consistent with a missing counterbalance spring.
parsed.cascading_damage: Bent connecting rod or motor strain

There is no conflicting evidence, making the diagnosis highly certain.

parsed.message:
The internal sacrificial anode of your geyser has been completely consumed, allowing the water to attack the steel tank wall. This has caused the tank to rust through, leaching iron oxide into the water and leaking directly into the drip tray. The loss of hot water duration and increased electricity usage happen because the heating element is constantly fighting to heat incoming cold water that is escaping through the leak.

This is a progressive failure that will eventually lead to a complete structural rupture of the cylinder. Operating a corroded tank also risks electrical short-circuiting if water reaches the electrical cover or thermostat housing.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will isolate the electrical supply at the main distribution board and shut off the cold water inlet valve. They will drain the remaining water from the failed cylinder, disconnect the plumbing fittings, and remove the damaged unit. New geyser will be installed in the drip tray, reconnected to the water and electrical lines, and commissioned with a new compliance certificate.

An under-sink or wall-cavity view showing copper and white plastic pipes passing through a wall, with a large dark water stain on the wooden shelf below.
An exterior view of an apartment building walkway showing the white overflow pipes running vertically down the wall near the window frame.

Install a replacement geyser and connect all hot, cold, and overflow pipework.
Test the electrical connections and issue a Certificate of Compliance.

parsed.message:
Your garage door is failing to open because the tension spring on the left-hand side is missing. On tilt-up wooden garage doors, these heavy springs work as a pair to counterbalance the immense weight of the timber. Without the left spring, the door is completely unbalanced, causing it to twist and bind in the tracks as it rises. 

When the automated opener attempts to lift this unbalanced load, it quickly detects the physical resistance. To prevent the motor from burning out or snapping the drive assembly, the opener's safety system halts operation, beeps an error code, and reverses the door back down. Operating the door in this condition puts extreme stress on the remaining spring and the motor carriage.

A full view of the wooden tilt-up door hanging crookedly, showing a large tension spring on the right side and none on the left.
A view looking up at the ceiling-mounted motor rail, showing the red emergency release cord and the trolley carriage connection.
A close-up of the silver mounting bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden door panel, showing the cotter pin securing the drawbar arm.

contractor_checklist: Supply and install a matching heavy-duty tension spring on the left side of the door.
Inspect the remaining right-hand spring for wear and tension balance.
Adjust and balance both springs so the door can be lifted easily by hand.
Test the motor's travel limits and safety obstruction settings to ensure smooth operation.

parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged via the red cord, try to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy and tilts to one side, the counterbalance spring is definitely compromised.
